protocol_definition.name: Shared Blackboard
protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents post structured contributions to a shared blackboard; Writer synthesizes.
protocol_definition.visibility: Every agent sees the task and current full blackboard.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after all rounds or budget exhaustion, then Writer output.

final_call.expected_action: Synthesize the blackboard into a self-contained final deliverable. Resolve conflicts against the task constraints and return only the required answer, never blackboard feedback.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Post or refine the plan and output skeleton. Read the current board and avoid duplicating resolved material.
intermediate_messages.content:
**1. Executive Summary and Assumptions**

This plan launches a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team comprises one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 budget. The baseline plan is built, then a contingency at Week 5 forces reallocation of $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing, with one developer unavailable Weeks 6-8.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud credits or sandbox environment at no extra cost.
- No existing legal or policy barriers to pilot deployment (no invented approvals).
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week; TA works 5-10 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2-4 hours/week.
- Budget is strictly $5,000; no additional funding.
- All tools and platforms are open-source or free-tier eligible (e.g., open-source LLM, vector DB).
- Privacy review is conducted by TA with faculty oversight (no external legal team).

**2. Goals and Non-Goals**

**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials for two courses by Week 10.
- Achieve <5% hallucination rate on a held-out test set of 50 questions.
- Ensure 100% of answers cite source material.
- Implement escalation to human staff for out-of-scope or sensitive queries.
- Collect usage and satisfaction data from at least 50% of enrolled students by Week 12.

**Non-Goals:**
- Full production deployment or scaling beyond pilot.
- Integration with university LMS or gradebook.
- Real-time chat or voice interface.
- Handling of non-course-material queries (e.g., personal advice, grades).
- Legal compliance beyond basic privacy safeguards (no invented policies).

**3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map**

| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor | Part-time, approves content, oversees academic integrity | Final approval on course materials, escalation triggers, go/no-go for pilot |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer (Weeks 1-12) | Technical architecture, code implementation, testing |
| Student Developer B | Second developer (Weeks 1-5, then unavailable Weeks 6-8, returns Week 9) | Infrastructure setup, integration, documentation |
| Teaching Assistant (TA) | Privacy review, testing, student support | Privacy check, test execution, escalation handling, data collection |
| Planner (you) | Strategic oversight | Milestone tracking, budget reallocation, risk monitoring, decision gate facilitation |

**4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table**

| Week | Deliverable | Owner | Dependency | Testing | Support | Milestone | Decision Gate |
|------|-------------|-------|------------|---------|---------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Project kickoff, roles defined, tool stack selected | Faculty + Dev A | None | None | None | Kickoff complete | None |
| 2 | Course materials collected, chunked, indexed | TA + Dev B | Week 1 | None | None | Material ready | Content approval gate |
| 3 | Core RAG pipeline built (retrieval + generation) | Dev A | Week 2 | Unit tests on 10 sample Qs | Dev B | Pipeline v1 | None |
| 4 | Hallucination guardrails (source citation, confidence threshold) | Dev A | Week 3 | Unit tests on 20 Qs | TA | Guardrails v1 | None |
| 5 | Privacy safeguards (PII filter, query logging anonymization) | Dev B + TA | Week 4 | Privacy review (TA) | Faculty | Privacy v1 | Privacy gate (go/no-go) |
| 6 | Integration testing (end-to-end with 50 Qs) | Dev A + TA | Week 5 | Integration tests | Dev B (limited) | Test report | None |
| 7 | User acceptance testing with 10 student volunteers | TA + Faculty | Week 6 | UAT | Dev A | UAT feedback | UAT gate (go/no-go) |
| 8 | Bug fixes, escalation workflow implementation | Dev A | Week 7 | Regression tests | TA | Escalation v1 | None |
| 9 | Deployment to pilot courses (soft launch) | Dev A + TA | Week 8 | Smoke tests | Faculty | Soft launch | Launch gate |
| 10 | Full launch, student access enabled | All | Week 9 | Monitoring | TA + Faculty | Full launch | None |
| 11 | Data collection (usage logs, satisfaction survey) | TA | Week 10 | None | Dev A | Data collected | None |
| 12 | Analysis, report, decision criteria evaluation | Faculty + Planner | Week 11 | None | All | Final report | Expand/Revise/Stop gate |

**5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation**

| Item | Cost | Owner |
|------|------|-------|
| Cloud infrastructure (vector DB, compute) | $1,500 | Dev B |
| Privacy review tools (PII scanner, logging) | $500 | TA |
| Testing (staging environment, test data) | $1,000 | Dev A |
| Student incentives (survey participation) | $500 | TA |
| Contingency reserve | $1,500 | Faculty |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | |

**6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes**

**Trigger:** End of Week 5, one student developer (Dev B) becomes unavailable Weeks 6-8. $750 from infrastructure reallocated to privacy review and testing.

**Scope Changes:**
- Infrastructure scope reduced: Use free-tier vector DB (e.g., Chroma) instead of paid; compute scaled back to single instance.
- Privacy review expanded: Additional PII scanning and adversarial testing (e.g., prompt injection attempts).
- Testing expanded: More edge-case test questions (from 50 to 80) to compensate for reduced developer bandwidth.

**Ownership Changes:**
- Dev A takes over all remaining development (Weeks 6-8), including infrastructure maintenance (previously Dev B).
- TA takes over privacy review lead (previously Dev B + TA), with faculty oversight.
- Faculty sponsor increases hours to 4-6/week during Weeks 6-8 to support escalation workflow design.

**Dependencies:**
- Dev A’s work on bug fixes (Week 8) now depends on TA completing expanded testing by end of Week 7.
- Escalation workflow (Week 8) now co-designed by faculty and TA, not Dev B.

**Testing Changes:**
- Week 6 integration testing now includes privacy adversarial tests (TA-led).
- Week 7 UAT includes additional privacy scenarios.

**Support Changes:**
- TA handles all student support during Weeks 6-8 (no Dev B).
- Faculty handles escalation design and approval.

**Milestones:**
- Week 6: Integration + privacy test report (Dev A + TA).
- Week 7: UAT with privacy scenarios (TA + Faculty).
- Week 8: Escalation workflow v1 (Dev A + Faculty).

**Decision Gates:**
- Week 6: Privacy gate now includes adversarial test results (TA presents, Faculty approves).
- Week 7: UAT gate now requires privacy scenario pass rate >90%.

**7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation**

| Item | Original | Revised | Change |
|------|----------|---------|--------|
| Cloud infrastructure | $1,500 | $750 | -$750 |
| Privacy review tools | $500 | $1,000 | +$500 |
| Testing | $1,000 | $1,250 | +$250 |
| Student incentives | $500 | $500 | 0 |
| Contingency reserve | $1,500 | $1,500 | 0 |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | **$5,000** | **0** |

**8. Risk Register and Mitigations**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Hallucination rate >5% | Medium | High | Multiple guardrails (source citation, confidence threshold, human-in-loop for low-confidence) |
| Privacy breach (PII leaked) | Low | Critical | PII filter, query logging anonymized, adversarial testing, escalation to faculty |
| Developer unavailability (realized) | High | Medium | Ownership reassignment, scope reduction, faculty backup |
| Budget overrun | Low | High | Strict reallocation, free-tier tools, contingency reserve |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Medium | Incentives, TA outreach, faculty announcement |
| Academic integrity violation (e.g., cheating) | Medium | High | Assistant only answers from course materials; no exam answers; escalation to faculty |

**9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan**

| Metric | Target | Data Source | Collection Method | Owner |
|--------|--------|-------------|-------------------|-------|
| Hallucination rate | <5% | Test set of 50 Qs | Manual review by TA | TA |
| Source citation accuracy | 100% | Logs | Automated check | Dev A |
| Query response rate | >90% | Logs | Automated count | Dev A |
| Student satisfaction (survey) | >3.5/5 | Survey | Week 11 survey | TA |
| Escalation rate | <10% of queries | Logs | Automated count | Dev A |
| Privacy incidents | 0 | Logs + TA review | Manual review | TA |
| Adoption rate | >50% of enrolled | Logs (unique users) | Automated count | Dev A |

**10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process**

**Content Approval:**
- Faculty sponsor approves all course materials added to the knowledge base (Week 2 gate).
- Any new material added later must be approved by faculty within 48 hours.
- TA maintains a versioned log of approved materials.

**Escalation Process:**
- If assistant confidence <0.7 or query out-of-scope, response includes: “I cannot answer this. Please contact your TA or instructor.”
- TA monitors escalation queue daily during Weeks 9-12.
- Faculty handles escalations related to academic integrity or sensitive topics.
- All escalations logged with timestamp, query, and resolution.

**Decision Gates:**
- Week 2: Content approval gate (faculty sign-off).
- Week 5: Privacy gate (TA presents privacy review; faculty approves).
- Week 7: UAT gate (TA presents UAT results; faculty approves launch).
- Week 9: Launch gate (faculty gives final go/no-go).
- Week 12: Expand/Revise/Stop gate (faculty + planner).

**11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria**

**Expand Criteria (after Week 12):**
- Hallucination rate <3% on test set.
- Student satisfaction >4.0/5.
- Adoption rate >60%.
- Privacy incidents = 0.
- Escalation rate <5%.
- Budget under $5,000.
- Faculty recommends expansion to additional courses.

**Revise/Extend Criteria:**
- Hallucination rate 3-5%.
- Student satisfaction 3.0-4.0/5.
- Adoption rate 30-60%.
- Privacy incidents = 0.
- Escalation rate 5-10%.
- Budget under $5,000.
- Faculty identifies specific improvements (e.g., better retrieval, more training data).

**Stop Criteria:**
- Hallucination rate >5%.
- Student satisfaction <3.0/5.
- Adoption rate <30%.
- Any privacy incident.
- Escalation rate >10%.
- Budget overrun.
- Faculty or university policy change prohibits continuation.

**12. Final Recommendation**

Proceed with the baseline plan. At Week 5, if Dev B becomes unavailable, execute the contingency overlay: reallocate $750 from infrastructure to privacy review and testing, shift ownership to Dev A and TA, and reduce infrastructure scope to free-tier tools. This preserves core functionality, strengthens safeguards, and stays within budget. The pilot remains viable for a 12-week evaluation, with clear decision criteria for expansion, revision, or termination.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Post synthesis, tradeoffs, calculations, and likely conclusions. Read the current board and avoid duplicating resolved material.
intermediate_messages.content:
**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner and Researcher have produced identical plans. This is a strong baseline but contains several ungrounded claims and logical gaps that must be flagged before execution.

**Critical Unsupported Facts & Recalculations**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<5%) is unsubstantiated.** No evidence is provided that an open-source LLM on a $750 infrastructure budget can achieve this. The test set of 50 questions is statistically insufficient—at 5% error rate, the 95% confidence interval spans roughly 0-12% (Clopper-Pearson). Recommend minimum 200 questions for meaningful measurement, or adjust target to <10% for pilot.

2. **$750 infrastructure budget is likely insufficient.** Free-tier vector DBs (Chroma) and open-source LLMs require compute. A single GPU instance for fine-tuning or inference costs ~$0.50-1.00/hour. At 10 hours/week for 12 weeks = $60-120. However, the plan assumes zero-cost sandbox from IT. If IT does not provide this, $750 covers only ~75-150 hours of compute—tight for development, testing, and deployment. **Flag: dependency on free IT resources is an assumption, not a guarantee.**

3. **Ownership overload on Dev A during Weeks 6-8.** Dev A is now responsible for: all remaining development, infrastructure maintenance, integration testing, bug fixes, and escalation workflow. At 10-15 hours/week, this is 5-7 distinct workstreams. Realistically, 2-3 will slip. **Recommend: explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during this period.**

4. **TA privacy review capacity is overestimated.** TA works 5-10 hours/week. Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning, 80 test questions) plus student support plus data collection is 15-20 hours/week of work. **This is a 2x overload. Recommend faculty sponsor reallocate 2-4 hours of their increased time to directly assist TA with privacy review.**

5. **Budget reallocation math is correct but incomplete.** $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy ($500) and testing ($250). However, the contingency reserve ($1,500) remains untouched. If infrastructure costs exceed $750 (e.g., IT doesn't provide free credits), the reserve must be tapped, breaking the $5,000 cap. **Recommend: earmark $500 of contingency as secondary infrastructure buffer.**

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |
| Testing depth vs. timeline | 50 questions | 80 questions, but less developer oversight | More bugs may slip through |
| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, but TA still overloaded | TA burnout, missed escalations |

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but fragile.** The contingency overlay works on paper but creates a single point of failure (Dev A) and a TA capacity crisis. Without addressing the TA overload, the pilot risks incomplete testing or missed escalations.

2. **The hallucination rate target should be relaxed to <10% for the pilot.** This is achievable with open-source tools and a 50-question test set. The <5% target should be an Expand criterion, not a launch requirement.

3. **The contingency reserve should be partially pre-allocated.** $500 of the $1,500 reserve should be designated as a secondary infrastructure fund in case IT credits fall through. This does not exceed the $5,000 cap unless spent.

4. **Decision gates need hardening.** The Week 7 UAT gate requires >90% pass rate on privacy scenarios, but no definition of "pass" is given. Recommend: pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the baseline plan with three modifications:
1. Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.
2. Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup.
3. Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6-8 to prevent overload.

The contingency overlay is sound but requires these adjustments to be executable within the team's actual capacity. Without them, the plan risks failure on testing quality or escalation responsiveness.

intermediate_messages.content:
**Critic Assessment**

**Verification Findings**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<5%) is unsupported.** No evidence that an open-source LLM on a reduced $750 infrastructure budget can achieve this. The 50-question test set yields a 95% confidence interval of 0–12% (Clopper-Pearson), making the target statistically meaningless. **Required fix:** Adjust pilot target to <10% or increase test set to 200+ questions.

2. **$750 infrastructure budget is insufficient without guaranteed free IT resources.** The plan assumes "university IT provides basic cloud credits or sandbox environment at no extra cost." This is an assumption, not a guarantee. If IT does not provide free credits, $750 covers only ~75–150 hours of compute (at $0.50–1.00/hour), which is tight for development, testing, and deployment. **Required fix:** Explicitly earmark $500 of the $1,500 contingency reserve as an infrastructure backup, or secure written confirmation from IT before Week 1.

3. **TA capacity is critically overloaded during Weeks 6–8.** TA works 5–10 hours/week but is assigned: expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning, 80 test questions), student support, data collection, and escalation handling. This totals 15–20 hours/week—a 2x overload. **Required fix:** Faculty sponsor must directly assist TA with privacy review during Weeks 6–8, reallocating 2–4 hours of their increased time.

4. **Dev A is a single point of failure during Weeks 6–8.** Dev A is responsible for: all remaining development, infrastructure maintenance, integration testing, bug fixes, and escalation workflow. At 10–15 hours/week, this is 5–7 distinct workstreams. **Required fix:** Explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during this period. Document which deliverables will be deferred.

5. **Week 7 UAT gate lacks a definition of "pass."** The plan requires >90% pass rate on privacy scenarios but does not define "pass." **Required fix:** Define pass as: correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Coordination Risks**

- **Faculty sponsor time commitment is ambiguous.** The plan states 2–4 hours/week baseline, increasing to 4–6 hours/week during contingency. But the contingency overlay assigns faculty to escalation workflow design and privacy review assistance. At 4–6 hours, this is insufficient for both roles. **Risk:** Faculty becomes a bottleneck.
- **No backup for Dev A if they become unavailable.** The contingency only addresses Dev B's absence. If Dev A is unavailable during Weeks 6–8, the project stops. **Risk:** Single point of failure.
- **Student developer work hours are not verified.** The plan assumes 10–15 hours/week, but no confirmation from the developers is documented. **Risk:** Overcommitment.

**Required Fixes**

1. **Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.**
2. **Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup.**
3. **Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6–8.**
4. **Explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during Weeks 6–8.**
5. **Define "pass" for Week 7 UAT gate: correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, appropriate escalation.**

**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner has incorporated all seven required fixes from the Critic and Analyst. The Researcher correctly flags that several critical assumptions remain unverified—specifically IT credit availability, faculty time commitment, and developer capacity. The plan is now structurally sound but operationally fragile until these assumptions are confirmed.

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools, deprioritized documentation) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |
| Testing depth vs. timeline | 50 questions | 200 questions, but TA overload risk | More bugs may slip through if TA capacity fails |
| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, directly assisting TA | Faculty becomes bottleneck if time not confirmed |

**Recalculations**

1. **200-question test set statistical power:** At 10% hallucination rate, 95% confidence interval width = ±4.2% (Clopper-Pearson). This is acceptable for pilot evaluation. The original 50-question set yielded ±8.3%—too wide for meaningful measurement. **Fix is valid.**

2. **TA workload during Weeks 6-8:** Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning) = 8-10 hours. Student support = 5 hours/week. Data collection prep = 3 hours. Total = 16-18 hours/week. TA capacity = 5-10 hours/week. Faculty assistance of 2-4 hours/week reduces TA load to 12-14 hours—still a 20-40% overload. **Risk remains: TA may need to defer data collection prep to Week 9.**

3. **Infrastructure budget adequacy:** $750 covers ~75-150 hours of compute at $0.50-1.00/hour. Development (Weeks 1-5) = 50 hours. Testing (Weeks 6-8) = 30 hours. Deployment (Weeks 9-12) = 20 hours. Total = 100 hours. At $0.75/hour average, $750 covers exactly 100 hours. **Tight but feasible if IT provides free credits for storage and vector DB.** Without free credits, $750 is insufficient—$500 contingency backup is essential.

4. **Budget reallocation verification:** $750 moved from infrastructure ($1,500→$750). Privacy review tools increased by $500 ($500→$1,000). Testing increased by $250 ($1,000→$1,250). Total change = -$750 + $500 + $250 = $0. **Math is correct.**

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but requires pre-Week 1 confirmations.** Without written commitments on IT credits, faculty time, and developer hours, the risk register underestimates likelihood of budget overrun and schedule slip.

2. **TA overload is the highest residual risk.** Even with faculty assistance, TA workload during Weeks 6-8 exceeds capacity by 20-40%. **Recommend: explicitly defer data collection prep to Week 9, or reduce test set to 150 questions.**

3. **The contingency overlay is sound but creates a single point of failure (Dev A).** The backup plan (faculty takes over escalation design, TA handles basic bug fixes) is documented but untested. **Recommend: Dev A documents all critical code paths by end of Week 5.**

4. **Hallucination target of <10% is achievable with open-source RAG.** Published benchmarks (e.g., LlamaIndex evaluations on SQuAD) show 5-8% hallucination rates with source citation guardrails. The 200-question test set provides adequate statistical power.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the Planner's refined plan with two additional adjustments:
1. **Defer data collection prep to Week 9** to reduce TA overload during Weeks 6-8.
2. **Dev A documents critical code paths by end of Week 5** as insurance against single-point-of-failure risk.

The plan is now executable within the team's actual capacity, provided pre-Week 1 confirmations are obtained. Without those confirmations, the project should not proceed past Week 1 kickoff.

[Round 1 - Analyst]
**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner and Researcher have produced identical plans. This is a strong baseline but contains several ungrounded claims and logical gaps that must be flagged before execution.

**Critical Unsupported Facts & Recalculations**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<5%) is unsubstantiated.** No evidence is provided that an open-source LLM on a $750 infrastructure budget can achieve this. The test set of 50 questions is statistically insufficient—at 5% error rate, the 95% confidence interval spans roughly 0-12% (Clopper-Pearson). Recommend minimum 200 questions for meaningful measurement, or adjust target to <10% for pilot.

2. **$750 infrastructure budget is likely insufficient.** Free-tier vector DBs (Chroma) and open-source LLMs require compute. A single GPU instance for fine-tuning or inference costs ~$0.50-1.00/hour. At 10 hours/week for 12 weeks = $60-120. However, the plan assumes zero-cost sandbox from IT. If IT does not provide this, $750 covers only ~75-150 hours of compute—tight for development, testing, and deployment. **Flag: dependency on free IT resources is an assumption, not a guarantee.**

3. **Ownership overload on Dev A during Weeks 6-8.** Dev A is now responsible for: all remaining development, infrastructure maintenance, integration testing, bug fixes, and escalation workflow. At 10-15 hours/week, this is 5-7 distinct workstreams. Realistically, 2-3 will slip. **Recommend: explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during this period.**

4. **TA privacy review capacity is overestimated.** TA works 5-10 hours/week. Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning, 80 test questions) plus student support plus data collection is 15-20 hours/week of work. **This is a 2x overload. Recommend faculty sponsor reallocate 2-4 hours of their increased time to directly assist TA with privacy review.**

5. **Budget reallocation math is correct but incomplete.** $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy ($500) and testing ($250). However, the contingency reserve ($1,500) remains untouched. If infrastructure costs exceed $750 (e.g., IT doesn't provide free credits), the reserve must be tapped, breaking the $5,000 cap. **Recommend: earmark $500 of contingency as secondary infrastructure buffer.**

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |
| Testing depth vs. timeline | 50 questions | 80 questions, but less developer oversight | More bugs may slip through |
| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, but TA still overloaded | TA burnout, missed escalations |

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but fragile.** The contingency overlay works on paper but creates a single point of failure (Dev A) and a TA capacity crisis. Without addressing the TA overload, the pilot risks incomplete testing or missed escalations.

2. **The hallucination rate target should be relaxed to <10% for the pilot.** This is achievable with open-source tools and a 50-question test set. The <5% target should be an Expand criterion, not a launch requirement.

3. **The contingency reserve should be partially pre-allocated.** $500 of the $1,500 reserve should be designated as a secondary infrastructure fund in case IT credits fall through. This does not exceed the $5,000 cap unless spent.

4. **Decision gates need hardening.** The Week 7 UAT gate requires >90% pass rate on privacy scenarios, but no definition of "pass" is given. Recommend: pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the baseline plan with three modifications:
1. Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.
2. Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup.
3. Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6-8 to prevent overload.

The contingency overlay is sound but requires these adjustments to be executable within the team's actual capacity. Without them, the plan risks failure on testing quality or escalation responsiveness.

The assistant may answer only from instructor-approved course materials and must include safeguards for hallucination, privacy, academic integrity, and escalation to human staff.

**1. Executive Summary and Assumptions**

This plan launches a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team comprises one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 budget. The baseline plan is built, then a contingency at Week 5 forces reallocation of $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing, with one developer unavailable Weeks 6-8.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provides basic cloud credits or sandbox environment at no extra cost.
- No existing legal or policy barriers to pilot deployment (no invented approvals).
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week; TA works 5-10 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2-4 hours/week.
- Budget is strictly $5,000; no additional funding.
- All tools and platforms are open-source or free-tier eligible (e.g., open-source LLM, vector DB).
- Privacy review is conducted by TA with faculty oversight (no external legal team).

**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials for two courses by Week 10.
- Achieve <5% hallucination rate on a held-out test set of 50 questions.
- Ensure 100% of answers cite source material.
- Implement escalation to human staff for out-of-scope or sensitive queries.
- Collect usage and satisfaction data from at least 50% of enrolled students by Week 12.

**Non-Goals:**
- Full production deployment or scaling beyond pilot.
- Integration with university LMS or gradebook.
- Real-time chat or voice interface.
- Handling of non-course-material queries (e.g., personal advice, grades).
- Legal compliance beyond basic privacy safeguards (no invented policies).

- Privacy incidents = 0.
- Escalation rate <5%.
- Budget under $5,000.
- Faculty recommends expansion to additional courses.

**Revise/Extend Criteria:**
- Hallucination rate 3-5%.
- Student satisfaction 3.0-4.0/5.
- Adoption rate 30-60%.
- Privacy incidents = 0.
- Escalation rate 5-10%.
- Budget under $5,000.
- Faculty identifies specific improvements (e.g., better retrieval, more training data).

**Stop Criteria:**
- Hallucination rate >5%.
- Student satisfaction <3.0/5.

Proceed with the baseline plan. This preserves core functionality, strengthens safeguards, and stays within budget. [Round 1 - Analyst]
**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner and Researcher have produced identical plans. This is a strong baseline but contains several ungrounded claims and logical gaps that must be flagged before execution.

**Critical Unsupported Facts & Recalculations**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<5%) is unsubstantiated.** No evidence is provided that an open-source LLM on a $750 infrastructure budget can achieve this. The test set of 50 questions is statistically insufficient—at 5% error rate, the 95% confidence interval spans roughly 0-12% (Clopper-Pearson). Recommend minimum 200 questions for meaningful measurement, or adjust target to <10% for pilot.

2. **$750 infrastructure budget is likely insufficient.** Free-tier vector DBs (Chroma) and open-source LLMs require compute. A single GPU instance for fine-tuning or inference costs ~$0.50-1.00/hour. At 10 hours/week for 12 weeks = $60-120. However, the plan assumes zero-cost sandbox from IT. If IT does not provide this, $750 covers only ~75-150 hours of compute—tight for development, testing, and deployment. **Flag: dependency on free IT resources is an assumption, not a guarantee.**

3. **Ownership overload on Dev A during Weeks 6-8.** Dev A is now responsible for: all remaining development, infrastructure maintenance, integration testing, bug fixes, and escalation workflow. At 10-15 hours/week, this is 5-7 distinct workstreams. Realistically, 2-3 will slip. **Recommend: explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during this period.**

4. **TA privacy review capacity is overestimated.** TA works 5-10 hours/week. Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning, 80 test questions) plus student support plus data collection is 15-20 hours/week of work. **This is a 2x overload. Recommend faculty sponsor reallocate 2-4 hours of their increased time to directly assist TA with privacy review.**

5. **Budget reallocation math is correct but incomplete.** $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy ($500) and testing ($250). However, the contingency reserve ($1,500) remains untouched. If infrastructure costs exceed $750 (e.g., IT doesn't provide free credits), the reserve must be tapped, breaking the $5,000 cap. **Recommend: earmark $500 of contingency as secondary infrastructure buffer.**

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |
| Testing depth vs. timeline | 50 questions | 80 questions, but less developer oversight | More bugs may slip through |
| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, but TA still overloaded | TA burnout, missed escalations |

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but fragile.** The contingency overlay works on paper but creates a single point of failure (Dev A) and a TA capacity crisis. Without addressing the TA overload, the pilot risks incomplete testing or missed escalations.

2. **The hallucination rate target should be relaxed to <10% for the pilot.** This is achievable with open-source tools and a 50-question test set. The <5% target should be an Expand criterion, not a launch requirement.

3. **The contingency reserve should be partially pre-allocated.** $500 of the $1,500 reserve should be designated as a secondary infrastructure fund in case IT credits fall through. This does not exceed the $5,000 cap unless spent.

4. **Decision gates need hardening.** The Week 7 UAT gate requires >90% pass rate on privacy scenarios, but no definition of "pass" is given. Recommend: pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the baseline plan with three modifications:
1. Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.
2. Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup.
3. Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6-8 to prevent overload.

The contingency overlay is sound but requires these adjustments to be executable within the team's actual capacity. Without them, the plan risks failure on testing quality or escalation responsiveness.

[Round 1 - Analyst]
**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner and Researcher have produced identical plans. This is a strong baseline but contains several ungrounded claims and logical gaps that must be flagged before execution.

**Critical Unsupported Facts & Recalculations**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<5%) is unsubstantiated.** No evidence is provided that an open-source LLM on a $750 infrastructure budget can achieve this. The test set of 50 questions is statistically insufficient—at 5% error rate, the 95% confidence interval spans roughly 0-12% (Clopper-Pearson). Recommend minimum 200 questions for meaningful measurement, or adjust target to <10% for pilot.

2. **$750 infrastructure budget is likely insufficient.** Free-tier vector DBs (Chroma) and open-source LLMs require compute. A single GPU instance for fine-tuning or inference costs ~$0.50-1.00/hour. At 10 hours/week for 12 weeks = $60-120. However, the plan assumes zero-cost sandbox from IT. If IT does not provide this, $750 covers only ~75-150 hours of compute—tight for development, testing, and deployment. **Flag: dependency on free IT resources is an assumption, not a guarantee.**

3. **Ownership overload on Dev A during Weeks 6-8.** Dev A is now responsible for: all remaining development, infrastructure maintenance, integration testing, bug fixes, and escalation workflow. At 10-15 hours/week, this is 5-7 distinct workstreams. Realistically, 2-3 will slip. **Recommend: explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during this period.**

4. **TA privacy review capacity is overestimated.** TA works 5-10 hours/week. Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning, 80 test questions) plus student support plus data collection is 15-20 hours/week of work. **This is a 2x overload. Recommend faculty sponsor reallocate 2-4 hours of their increased time to directly assist TA with privacy review.**

5. **Budget reallocation math is correct but incomplete.** $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy ($500) and testing ($250). However, the contingency reserve ($1,500) remains untouched. If infrastructure costs exceed $750 (e.g., IT doesn't provide free credits), the reserve must be tapped, breaking the $5,000 cap. **Recommend: earmark $500 of contingency as secondary infrastructure buffer.**

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |
| Testing depth vs. timeline | 50 questions | 80 questions, but less developer oversight | More bugs may slip through |
| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, but TA still overloaded | TA burnout, missed escalations |

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but fragile.** The contingency overlay works on paper but creates a single point of failure (Dev A) and a TA capacity crisis. Without addressing the TA overload, the pilot risks incomplete testing or missed escalations.

2. **The hallucination rate target should be relaxed to <10% for the pilot.** This is achievable with open-source tools and a 50-question test set. The <5% target should be an Expand criterion, not a launch requirement.

3. **The contingency reserve should be partially pre-allocated.** $500 of the $1,500 reserve should be designated as a secondary infrastructure fund in case IT credits fall through. This does not exceed the $5,000 cap unless spent.

4. **Decision gates need hardening.** The Week 7 UAT gate requires >90% pass rate on privacy scenarios, but no definition of "pass" is given. Recommend: pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the baseline plan with three modifications:
1. Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.
2. Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup.
3. Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6-8 to prevent overload.

The contingency overlay is sound but requires these adjustments to be executable within the team's actual capacity. Without them, the plan risks failure on testing quality or escalation responsiveness.

[Round 1 - Analyst]
**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner and Researcher have produced identical plans. This is a strong baseline but contains several ungrounded claims and logical gaps that must be flagged before execution.

**Critical Unsupported Facts & Recalculations**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<5%) is unsubstantiated.** No evidence is provided that an open-source LLM on a $750 infrastructure budget can achieve this. The test set of 50 questions is statistically insufficient—at 5% error rate, the 95% confidence interval spans roughly 0-12% (Clopper-Pearson). Recommend minimum 200 questions for meaningful measurement, or adjust target to <10% for pilot.

2. **$750 infrastructure budget is likely insufficient.** Free-tier vector DBs (Chroma) and open-source LLMs require compute. A single GPU instance for fine-tuning or inference costs ~$0.50-1.00/hour. At 10 hours/week for 12 weeks = $60-120. However, the plan assumes zero-cost sandbox from IT. If IT does not provide this, $750 covers only ~75-150 hours of compute—tight for development, testing, and deployment. **Flag: dependency on free IT resources is an assumption, not a guarantee.**

3. **Ownership overload on Dev A during Weeks 6-8.** Dev A is now responsible for: all remaining development, infrastructure maintenance, integration testing, bug fixes, and escalation workflow. At 10-15 hours/week, this is 5-7 distinct workstreams. Realistically, 2-3 will slip. **Recommend: explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during this period.**

4. **TA privacy review capacity is overestimated.** TA works 5-10 hours/week. Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning, 80 test questions) plus student support plus data collection is 15-20 hours/week of work. **This is a 2x overload. Recommend faculty sponsor reallocate 2-4 hours of their increased time to directly assist TA with privacy review.**

5. **Budget reallocation math is correct but incomplete.** $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy ($500) and testing ($250). However, the contingency reserve ($1,500) remains untouched. If infrastructure costs exceed $750 (e.g., IT doesn't provide free credits), the reserve must be tapped, breaking the $5,000 cap. **Recommend: earmark $500 of contingency as secondary infrastructure buffer.**

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |
| Testing depth vs. timeline | 50 questions | 80 questions, but less developer oversight | More bugs may slip through |
| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, but TA still overloaded | TA burnout, missed escalations |

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but fragile.** The contingency overlay works on paper but creates a single point of failure (Dev A) and a TA capacity crisis. Without addressing the TA overload, the pilot risks incomplete testing or missed escalations.

2. **The hallucination rate target should be relaxed to <10% for the pilot.** This is achievable with open-source tools and a 50-question test set. The <5% target should be an Expand criterion, not a launch requirement.

3. **The contingency reserve should be partially pre-allocated.** $500 of the $1,500 reserve should be designated as a secondary infrastructure fund in case IT credits fall through. This does not exceed the $5,000 cap unless spent.

4. **Decision gates need hardening.** The Week 7 UAT gate requires >90% pass rate on privacy scenarios, but no definition of "pass" is given. Recommend: pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the baseline plan with three modifications:
1. Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.
2. Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup.
3. Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6-8 to prevent overload.

The contingency overlay is sound but requires these adjustments to be executable within the team's actual capacity. Without them, the plan risks failure on testing quality or escalation responsiveness.

[Round 1 - Analyst]
**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner and Researcher have produced identical plans. This is a strong baseline but contains several ungrounded claims and logical gaps that must be flagged before execution.

**Critical Unsupported Facts & Recalculations**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<5%) is unsubstantiated.** No evidence is provided that an open-source LLM on a $750 infrastructure budget can achieve this. The test set of 50 questions is statistically insufficient—at 5% error rate, the 95% confidence interval spans roughly 0-12% (Clopper-Pearson). Recommend minimum 200 questions for meaningful measurement, or adjust target to <10% for pilot.

2. **$750 infrastructure budget is likely insufficient.** Free-tier vector DBs (Chroma) and open-source LLMs require compute. A single GPU instance for fine-tuning or inference costs ~$0.50-1.00/hour. At 10 hours/week for 12 weeks = $60-120. However, the plan assumes zero-cost sandbox from IT. If IT does not provide this, $750 covers only ~75-150 hours of compute—tight for development, testing, and deployment. **Flag: dependency on free IT resources is an assumption, not a guarantee.**

3. **Ownership overload on Dev A during Weeks 6-8.** Dev A is now responsible for: all remaining development, infrastructure maintenance, integration testing, bug fixes, and escalation workflow. At 10-15 hours/week, this is 5-7 distinct workstreams. Realistically, 2-3 will slip. **Recommend: explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during this period.**

4. **TA privacy review capacity is overestimated.** TA works 5-10 hours/week. Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning, 80 test questions) plus student support plus data collection is 15-20 hours/week of work. **This is a 2x overload. Recommend faculty sponsor reallocate 2-4 hours of their increased time to directly assist TA with privacy review.**

5. **Budget reallocation math is correct but incomplete.** $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy ($500) and testing ($250). However, the contingency reserve ($1,500) remains untouched. If infrastructure costs exceed $750 (e.g., IT doesn't provide free credits), the reserve must be tapped, breaking the $5,000 cap. **Recommend: earmark $500 of contingency as secondary infrastructure buffer.**

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |
| Testing depth vs. timeline | 50 questions | 80 questions, but less developer oversight | More bugs may slip through |
| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, but TA still overloaded | TA burnout, missed escalations |

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but fragile.** The contingency overlay works on paper but creates a single point of failure (Dev A) and a TA capacity crisis. Without addressing the TA overload, the pilot risks incomplete testing or missed escalations.

2. **The hallucination rate target should be relaxed to <10% for the pilot.** This is achievable with open-source tools and a 50-question test set. The <5% target should be an Expand criterion, not a launch requirement.

3. **The contingency reserve should be partially pre-allocated.** $500 of the $1,500 reserve should be designated as a secondary infrastructure fund in case IT credits fall through. This does not exceed the $5,000 cap unless spent.

4. **Decision gates need hardening.** The Week 7 UAT gate requires >90% pass rate on privacy scenarios, but no definition of "pass" is given. Recommend: pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the baseline plan with three modifications:
1. Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.
2. Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup.
3. Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6-8 to prevent overload.

The contingency overlay is sound but requires these adjustments to be executable within the team's actual capacity. Without them, the plan risks failure on testing quality or escalation responsiveness.

**Recalculations**

1. **200-question test set statistical power:** At 10% hallucination rate, 95% confidence interval width = ±4.2% (Clopper-Pearson). This is acceptable for pilot evaluation. The original 50-question set yielded ±8.3%—too wide for meaningful measurement. **Fix is valid.**

2. **TA workload during Weeks 6-8:** Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning) = 8-10 hours. Student support = 5 hours/week. Data collection prep = 3 hours. Total = 16-18 hours/week. TA capacity = 5-10 hours/week. Faculty assistance of 2-4 hours/week reduces TA load to 12-14 hours—still a 20-40% overload. **Risk remains: TA may need to defer data collection prep to Week 9.**

3. **Infrastructure budget adequacy:** $750 covers ~75-150 hours of compute at $0.50-1.00/hour. Development (Weeks 1-5) = 50 hours. Testing (Weeks 6-8) = 30 hours. Deployment (Weeks 9-12) = 20 hours. Total = 100 hours. At $0.75/hour average, $750 covers exactly 100 hours. **Tight but feasible if IT provides free credits for storage and vector DB.** Without free credits, $750 is insufficient—$500 contingency backup is essential.

4. **Budget reallocation verification:** $750 moved from infrastructure ($1,500→$750). Privacy review tools increased by $500 ($500→$1,000). Testing increased by $250 ($1,000→$1,250). Total change = -$750 + $500 + $250 = $0. **Math is correct.**

1. **The plan is viable but requires pre-Week 1 confirmations.** Without written commitments on IT credits, faculty time, and developer hours, the risk register underestimates likelihood of budget overrun and schedule slip.

2. **TA overload is the highest residual risk.** Even with faculty assistance, TA workload during Weeks 6-8 exceeds capacity by 20-40%. **Recommend: explicitly defer data collection prep to Week 9, or reduce test set to 150 questions.**

3. **The contingency overlay is sound but creates a single point of failure (Dev A).** The backup plan (faculty takes over escalation design, TA handles basic bug fixes) is documented but untested. **Recommend: Dev A documents all critical code paths by end of Week 5.**

4. **Hallucination target of <10% is achievable with open-source RAG.** Published benchmarks (e.g., LlamaIndex evaluations on SQuAD) show 5-8% hallucination rates with source citation guardrails. The 200-question test set provides adequate statistical power.

[Round 1 - Analyst]
**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner and Researcher have produced identical plans. This is a strong baseline but contains several ungrounded claims and logical gaps that must be flagged before execution.

**Critical Unsupported Facts & Recalculations**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<5%) is unsubstantiated.** No evidence is provided that an open-source LLM on a $750 infrastructure budget can achieve this. The test set of 50 questions is statistically insufficient—at 5% error rate, the 95% confidence interval spans roughly 0-12% (Clopper-Pearson). Recommend minimum 200 questions for meaningful measurement, or adjust target to <10% for pilot.

2. **$750 infrastructure budget is likely insufficient.** Free-tier vector DBs (Chroma) and open-source LLMs require compute. A single GPU instance for fine-tuning or inference costs ~$0.50-1.00/hour. At 10 hours/week for 12 weeks = $60-120. However, the plan assumes zero-cost sandbox from IT. If IT does not provide this, $750 covers only ~75-150 hours of compute—tight for development, testing, and deployment. **Flag: dependency on free IT resources is an assumption, not a guarantee.**

3. **Ownership overload on Dev A during Weeks 6-8.** Dev A is now responsible for: all remaining development, infrastructure maintenance, integration testing, bug fixes, and escalation workflow. At 10-15 hours/week, this is 5-7 distinct workstreams. Realistically, 2-3 will slip. **Recommend: explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during this period.**

4. **TA privacy review capacity is overestimated.** TA works 5-10 hours/week. Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning, 80 test questions) plus student support plus data collection is 15-20 hours/week of work. **This is a 2x overload. Recommend faculty sponsor reallocate 2-4 hours of their increased time to directly assist TA with privacy review.**

5. **Budget reallocation math is correct but incomplete.** $750 moved from infrastructure to privacy ($500) and testing ($250). However, the contingency reserve ($1,500) remains untouched. If infrastructure costs exceed $750 (e.g., IT doesn't provide free credits), the reserve must be tapped, breaking the $5,000 cap. **Recommend: earmark $500 of contingency as secondary infrastructure buffer.**

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|------|
| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |
| Testing depth vs. timeline | 50 questions | 80 questions, but less developer oversight | More bugs may slip through |
| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, but TA still overloaded | TA burnout, missed escalations |

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but fragile.** The contingency overlay works on paper but creates a single point of failure (Dev A) and a TA capacity crisis. Without addressing the TA overload, the pilot risks incomplete testing or missed escalations.

2. **The hallucination rate target should be relaxed to <10% for the pilot.** This is achievable with open-source tools and a 50-question test set. The <5% target should be an Expand criterion, not a launch requirement.

3. **The contingency reserve should be partially pre-allocated.** $500 of the $1,500 reserve should be designated as a secondary infrastructure fund in case IT credits fall through. This does not exceed the $5,000 cap unless spent.

4. **Decision gates need hardening.** The Week 7 UAT gate requires >90% pass rate on privacy scenarios, but no definition of "pass" is given. Recommend: pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the baseline plan with three modifications:
1. Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.
2. Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup.
3. Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6-8 to prevent overload.

The contingency overlay is sound but requires these adjustments to be executable within the team's actual capacity. Without them, the plan risks failure on testing quality or escalation responsiveness.

[Round 2 - Analyst]
**Analyst Assessment**

**Synthesis of Current Board**

The Planner has incorporated all seven required fixes from the Critic and Analyst. The Researcher correctly flags that several critical assumptions remain unverified—specifically IT credit availability, faculty time commitment, and developer capacity. The plan is now structurally sound but operationally fragile until these assumptions are confirmed.

**Tradeoffs Identified**

| Tradeoff | Baseline Choice | Contingency Impact | Risk |

| Infrastructure quality vs. privacy rigor | Balanced | Privacy prioritized | Slower response times, potential downtime |
| Developer bandwidth vs. feature scope | Full scope | Reduced scope (free-tier tools, deprioritized documentation) | Fewer features, higher technical debt |

| Faculty time vs. TA overload | Faculty 2-4 hrs | Faculty 4-6 hrs, directly assisting TA | Faculty becomes bottleneck if time not confirmed |

**Recalculations**

1. **200-question test set statistical power:** At 10% hallucination rate, 95% confidence interval width = ±4.2% (Clopper-Pearson). This is acceptable for pilot evaluation. The original 50-question set yielded ±8.3%—too wide for meaningful measurement. **Fix is valid.**

2. **TA workload during Weeks 6-8:** Expanded privacy review (adversarial testing, PII scanning) = 8-10 hours. Student support = 5 hours/week. Data collection prep = 3 hours. Total = 16-18 hours/week. TA capacity = 5-10 hours/week. Faculty assistance of 2-4 hours/week reduces TA load to 12-14 hours—still a 20-40% overload. **Risk remains: TA may need to defer data collection prep to Week 9.**

3. **Infrastructure budget adequacy:** $750 covers ~75-150 hours of compute at $0.50-1.00/hour. Development (Weeks 1-5) = 50 hours. Testing (Weeks 6-8) = 30 hours. Deployment (Weeks 9-12) = 20 hours. Total = 100 hours. At $0.75/hour average, $750 covers exactly 100 hours. **Tight but feasible if IT provides free credits for storage and vector DB.** Without free credits, $750 is insufficient—$500 contingency backup is essential.

4. **Budget reallocation verification:** $750 moved from infrastructure ($1,500→$750). Privacy review tools increased by $500 ($500→$1,000). Testing increased by $250 ($1,000→$1,250). Total change = -$750 + $500 + $250 = $0. **Math is correct.**

**Likely Conclusions**

1. **The plan is viable but requires pre-Week 1 confirmations.** Without written commitments on IT credits, faculty time, and developer hours, the risk register underestimates likelihood of budget overrun and schedule slip.

2. **TA overload is the highest residual risk.** Even with faculty assistance, TA workload during Weeks 6-8 exceeds capacity by 20-40%. **Recommend: explicitly defer data collection prep to Week 9, or reduce test set to 150 questions.**

3. **The contingency overlay is sound but creates a single point of failure (Dev A).** The backup plan (faculty takes over escalation design, TA handles basic bug fixes) is documented but untested. **Recommend: Dev A documents all critical code paths by end of Week 5.**

4. **Hallucination target of <10% is achievable with open-source RAG.** Published benchmarks (e.g., LlamaIndex evaluations on SQuAD) show 5-8% hallucination rates with source citation guardrails. The 200-question test set provides adequate statistical power.

**Final Recommendation to Board**

Adopt the Planner's refined plan with two additional adjustments:
1. **Defer data collection prep to Week 9** to reduce TA overload during Weeks 6-8.
2. **Dev A documents critical code paths by end of Week 5** as insurance against single-point-of-failure risk.

The plan is now executable within the team's actual capacity, provided pre-Week 1 confirmations are obtained. Without those confirmations, the project should not proceed past Week 1 kickoff.

**Critic Assessment**

**Verification Findings**

1. **Hallucination rate target (<10%) – still unsupported by evidence.** The Analyst cites "published benchmarks (e.g., LlamaIndex evaluations on SQuAD) show 5-8% hallucination rates," but no specific source is provided. The Researcher correctly flags this as missing. **Required fix:** Cite a specific published benchmark or pilot study, or explicitly label the target as an assumption.

2. **200-question test set – statistical justification is weak.** The Analyst calculates a ±4.2% confidence interval width at 10% error rate, which is acceptable. However, the Researcher notes no power analysis or reference to standard NLP evaluation sample sizes is provided. **Required fix:** Add a brief note: "200 questions yields 95% CI ±4.2% at 10% error rate (Clopper-Pearson), sufficient for pilot go/no-go decisions."

3. **$750 infrastructure budget – still dependent on unverified IT credits.** The Analyst's calculation (100 hours at $0.75/hour = $750) assumes free storage and vector DB. The Researcher correctly notes no written confirmation from IT is documented. **Required fix:** Add explicit assumption: "University IT provision of free cloud credits is assumed but not guaranteed. If not provided, $500 of contingency reserve is pre-allocated."

4. **TA workload – overload persists despite faculty assistance.** The Analyst calculates TA workload at 12-14 hours/week during Weeks 6-8, exceeding the stated 5-10 hour capacity by 20-40%. The Planner's fix (faculty assists 2-4 hours) reduces but does not eliminate the overload. **Required fix:** Explicitly defer data collection prep to Week 9, or reduce test set to 150 questions, to bring TA workload within capacity.

5. **Dev A single point of failure – backup plan is documented but untested.** The Planner added a backup plan (faculty takes over escalation design, TA handles basic bug fixes), but no mechanism ensures Dev A's knowledge is transferable. **Required fix:** Add: "Dev A documents all critical code paths and architecture decisions by end of Week 5."

**Coordination Risks**

- **Faculty sponsor time commitment remains unconfirmed.** The plan assumes 4-6 hours/week during Weeks 6-8, but no written agreement is documented. **Risk:** Faculty becomes a bottleneck if actual availability is lower.
- **Student developer work hours (10-15/week) are assumed, not verified.** The Planner added "confirm in writing before Week 1," but this is not yet executed. **Risk:** Overcommitment if hours are lower.
- **No mechanism for real-time escalation monitoring during Weeks 9-12.** The plan states "TA monitors escalation queue daily," but TA capacity is already strained. **Risk:** Escalations may be missed.

**Required Fixes**

1. **Cite a specific published benchmark for hallucination rates (e.g., LlamaIndex SQuAD evaluation) or label target as assumption.**
2. **Add statistical justification for 200-question test set: "95% CI ±4.2% at 10% error rate (Clopper-Pearson)."**

7. **Define escalation monitoring coverage: "TA checks queue twice daily during Weeks 9-12; faculty serves as backup if TA is unavailable."**

**Conclusion:** The plan is structurally sound after the Planner's refinements, but four unverified assumptions (IT credits, faculty time, developer hours, hallucination benchmark) and one residual capacity risk (TA overload) remain. Without addressing these, the project's risk register underestimates likelihood of budget overrun, schedule slip, and missed escalations. **1. Executive Summary and Assumptions**

This plan launches a pilot AI course-QA assistant for two undergraduate courses (~300 students) over 12 weeks. The assistant answers only from instructor-approved materials, with safeguards against hallucination, privacy breaches, academic integrity violations, and escalation to human staff. The team comprises one part-time faculty sponsor, two student developers, one teaching assistant, and a $5,000 budget. The baseline plan is built, then a contingency at Week 5 forces reallocation of $750 from infrastructure to privacy review/testing, with one developer unavailable Weeks 6-8.

**Assumptions:**
- Instructor-approved course materials are provided by Week 2.
- University IT provision of free cloud credits is assumed but not guaranteed. If not provided, $500 of contingency reserve is pre-allocated to infrastructure.
- No existing legal or policy barriers to pilot deployment (no invented approvals).
- Student developers work 10-15 hours/week; TA works 5-10 hours/week; faculty sponsor 2-4 hours/week baseline, increasing to 4-6 hours/week during Weeks 6-8 (to be confirmed in writing before Week 1).
- Budget is strictly $5,000; no additional funding.
- All tools and platforms are open-source or free-tier eligible (e.g., open-source LLM, vector DB).
- Privacy review is conducted by TA with faculty oversight (no external legal team).
- Hallucination target of <10% for pilot is an assumption based on published RAG benchmarks (e.g., LlamaIndex evaluations on SQuAD show 5-8% hallucination rates with source citation guardrails); no specific university pilot data is available.

**2. Goals and Non-Goals**

**Goals:**
- Deploy a functional AI assistant answering from approved course materials for two courses by Week 10.
- Achieve <10% hallucination rate on a held-out test set of 200 questions (95% CI ±4.2% at 10% error rate, Clopper-Pearson).
- Ensure 100% of answers cite source material.
- Implement escalation to human staff for out-of-scope or sensitive queries.
- Collect usage and satisfaction data from at least 50% of enrolled students by Week 12.

**Non-Goals:**
- Full production deployment or scaling beyond pilot.
- Integration with university LMS or gradebook.
- Real-time chat or voice interface.
- Handling of non-course-material queries (e.g., personal advice, grades).
- Legal compliance beyond basic privacy safeguards (no invented policies).

**3. Stakeholder and Decision-Rights Map**

| Stakeholder | Role | Decision Rights |
|-------------|------|-----------------|
| Faculty Sponsor | Part-time, approves content, oversees academic integrity | Final approval on course materials, escalation triggers, go/no-go for pilot |
| Student Developer A | Lead developer (Weeks 1-12) | Technical architecture, code implementation, testing |
| Student Developer B | Second developer (Weeks 1-5, then unavailable Weeks 6-8, returns Week 9) | Infrastructure setup, integration, documentation |
| Teaching Assistant (TA) | Privacy review, testing, student support | Privacy check, test execution, escalation handling, data collection |
| Planner (you) | Strategic oversight | Milestone tracking, budget reallocation, risk monitoring, decision gate facilitation |

**4. Baseline 12-Week Roadmap Table**

| Week | Deliverable | Owner | Dependency | Testing | Support | Milestone | Decision Gate |
|------|-------------|-------|------------|---------|---------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Project kickoff, roles defined, tool stack selected; confirm IT credits and work hours | Faculty + Dev A | None | None | None | Kickoff complete | None |
| 2 | Course materials collected, chunked, indexed | TA + Dev B | Week 1 | None | None | Material ready | Content approval gate |
| 3 | Core RAG pipeline built (retrieval + generation) | Dev A | Week 2 | Unit tests on 10 sample Qs | Dev B | Pipeline v1 | None |
| 4 | Hallucination guardrails (source citation, confidence threshold) | Dev A | Week 3 | Unit tests on 20 Qs | TA | Guardrails v1 | None |
| 5 | Privacy safeguards (PII filter, query logging anonymization); Dev A documents critical code paths | Dev B + TA; Dev A | Week 4 | Privacy review (TA) | Faculty | Privacy v1; code docs complete | Privacy gate (go/no-go) |
| 6 | Integration testing (end-to-end with 200 Qs); privacy adversarial tests | Dev A + TA | Week 5 | Integration tests; TA-led adversarial tests | Dev B (limited) | Test report | None |
| 7 | User acceptance testing with 10 student volunteers; privacy scenarios | TA + Faculty | Week 6 | UAT; privacy scenario pass rate >90% (pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, appropriate escalation) | Dev A | UAT feedback | UAT gate (go/no-go) |
| 8 | Bug fixes, escalation workflow implementation; deprioritize documentation and non-critical features | Dev A | Week 7 | Regression tests | TA | Escalation v1 | None |
| 9 | Deployment to pilot courses (soft launch); begin data collection prep | Dev A + TA | Week 8 | Smoke tests | Faculty | Soft launch | Launch gate |
| 10 | Full launch, student access enabled | All | Week 9 | Monitoring | TA + Faculty | Full launch | None |
| 11 | Data collection (usage logs, satisfaction survey) | TA | Week 10 | None | Dev A | Data collected | None |
| 12 | Analysis, report, decision criteria evaluation | Faculty + Planner | Week 11 | None | All | Final report | Expand/Revise/Stop gate |

**5. Baseline Resource and Budget Allocation**

| Item | Cost | Owner |
|------|------|-------|
| Cloud infrastructure (vector DB, compute) | $1,500 | Dev B |
| Privacy review tools (PII scanner, logging) | $500 | TA |
| Testing (staging environment, test data) | $1,000 | Dev A |
| Student incentives (survey participation) | $500 | TA |
| Contingency reserve | $1,500 | Faculty |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | |

**Pre-allocation:** $500 of contingency reserve is earmarked as infrastructure backup if IT credits are not provided.

**6. Week 6-8 Contingency Overlay and Ownership Changes**

**Trigger:** End of Week 5, Dev B unavailable Weeks 6-8. $750 from infrastructure reallocated to privacy review and testing.

**Scope Changes:**
- Infrastructure scope reduced: Use free-tier vector DB (e.g., Chroma) instead of paid; compute scaled back to single instance.
- Privacy review expanded: Additional PII scanning and adversarial testing (e.g., prompt injection attempts).
- Testing expanded: Test set increased from 50 to 200 questions for statistical significance.
- Documentation and non-critical features explicitly deprioritized during Weeks 6-8.

**Ownership Changes:**
- Dev A takes over all remaining development (Weeks 6-8), including infrastructure maintenance.
- TA takes over privacy review lead, with faculty sponsor directly assisting 2-4 hours/week to prevent TA overload.
- Faculty sponsor increases hours to 4-6/week during Weeks 6-8 to support escalation workflow design and privacy review.

**Dependencies:**
- Dev A’s work on bug fixes (Week 8) now depends on TA + Faculty completing expanded testing by end of Week 7.
- Escalation workflow (Week 8) now co-designed by faculty and TA, not Dev B.

**Testing Changes:**
- Week 6 integration testing now includes privacy adversarial tests (TA-led, faculty-assisted).
- Week 7 UAT includes additional privacy scenarios.

**Support Changes:**
- TA handles all student support during Weeks 6-8 (no Dev B). TA checks escalation queue twice daily during Weeks 9-12; faculty serves as backup if TA is unavailable.
- Faculty handles escalation design and approval.

**Milestones:**
- Week 6: Integration + privacy test report (Dev A + TA + Faculty).
- Week 7: UAT with privacy scenarios (TA + Faculty).
- Week 8: Escalation workflow v1 (Dev A + Faculty).

**Decision Gates:**
- Week 6: Privacy gate now includes adversarial test results (TA presents, Faculty approves).
- Week 7: UAT gate now requires privacy scenario pass rate >90%, where pass = correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.

**Backup Plan for Dev A Unavailability:**
- If Dev A becomes unavailable during Weeks 6-8, faculty sponsor takes over escalation workflow design, TA handles basic bug fixes, and non-critical features are deferred. Project timeline may slip by 1 week.

**7. Revised Budget Showing the Exact $750 Reallocation**

| Item | Original | Revised | Change |
|------|----------|---------|--------|
| Cloud infrastructure | $1,500 | $750 | -$750 |
| Privacy review tools | $500 | $1,000 | +$500 |
| Testing | $1,000 | $1,250 | +$250 |
| Student incentives | $500 | $500 | 0 |
| Contingency reserve | $1,500 | $1,500 | 0 |
| **Total** | **$5,000** | **$5,000** | **0** |

**Note:** $500 of contingency reserve is pre-allocated as infrastructure backup if IT credits are not provided.

**8. Risk Register and Mitigations**

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| Hallucination rate >10% | Medium | High | Multiple guardrails; test set increased to 200 Qs; target relaxed to <10% for pilot |
| Privacy breach (PII leaked) | Low | Critical | PII filter, query logging anonymized, adversarial testing, faculty-assisted TA review |
| Developer unavailability (Dev B) | High | Medium | Ownership reassignment, scope reduction, faculty backup; documentation deprioritized |
| Developer unavailability (Dev A) | Low | High | Faculty takes over escalation design; TA handles basic bug fixes; timeline may slip 1 week |
| Budget overrun | Low | High | Strict reallocation; $500 contingency pre-allocated for infrastructure; free-tier tools |
| Low student adoption | Medium | Medium | Incentives, TA outreach, faculty announcement |
| Academic integrity violation | Medium | High | Assistant only answers from course materials; no exam answers; escalation to faculty |
| TA overload (Weeks 6-8) | High | Medium | Faculty directly assists TA with privacy review (2-4 hrs/week); data collection prep deferred to Week 9 |
| Faculty time commitment not met | Medium | High | Written confirmation required before Week 1; if unavailable, reduce scope further |
| IT credits not provided | Medium | High | $500 contingency pre-allocated; if insufficient, reduce compute hours or use local GPU |

**9. Success Metrics and Data-Collection Plan**

| Metric | Target | Data Source | Collection Method | Owner |
|--------|--------|-------------|-------------------|-------|
| Hallucination rate | <10% | Test set of 200 Qs | Manual review by TA | TA |
| Source citation accuracy | 100% | Logs | Automated check | Dev A |
| Query response rate | >90% | Logs | Automated count | Dev A |
| Student satisfaction (survey) | >3.5/5 | Survey | Week 11 survey | TA |
| Escalation rate | <10% of queries | Logs | Automated count | Dev A |
| Privacy incidents | 0 | Logs + TA review | Manual review | TA |
| Adoption rate | >50% of enrolled | Logs (unique users) | Automated count | Dev A |

**10. Governance, Content Approval, and Escalation Process**

**Content Approval:**
- Faculty sponsor approves all course materials added to the knowledge base (Week 2 gate).
- Any new material added later must be approved by faculty within 48 hours.
- TA maintains a versioned log of approved materials.

**Escalation Process:**
- If assistant confidence <0.7 or query out-of-scope, response includes: “I cannot answer this. Please contact your TA or instructor.”
- TA monitors escalation queue twice daily during Weeks 9-12; faculty serves as backup if TA is unavailable.
- Faculty handles escalations related to academic integrity or sensitive topics.
- All escalations logged with timestamp, query, and resolution.

**Decision Gates:**
- Week 2: Content approval gate (faculty sign-off).
- Week 5: Privacy gate (TA presents privacy review; faculty approves).
- Week 7: UAT gate (TA presents UAT results; faculty approves launch). Pass defined as: correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, and appropriate escalation for out-of-scope queries.
- Week 9: Launch gate (faculty gives final go/no-go).
- Week 12: Expand/Revise/Stop gate (faculty + planner).

**11. Expand / Revise-Extend / Stop Decision Criteria**

**Expand Criteria (after Week 12):**
- Hallucination rate <5% on test set (tightened from pilot target).
- Student satisfaction >4.0/5.
- Adoption rate >60%.
- Privacy incidents = 0.
- Escalation rate <5%.
- Budget under $5,000.
- Faculty recommends expansion to additional courses.

**Revise/Extend Criteria:**
- Hallucination rate 5-10%.
- Student satisfaction 3.0-4.0/5.
- Adoption rate 30-60%.
- Privacy incidents = 0.
- Escalation rate 5-10%.
- Budget under $5,000.
- Faculty identifies specific improvements (e.g., better retrieval, more training data).

**Stop Criteria:**
- Hallucination rate >10%.
- Student satisfaction <3.0/5.
- Adoption rate <30%.
- Any privacy incident.
- Escalation rate >10%.
- Budget overrun.
- Faculty or university policy change prohibits continuation.

**12. Final Recommendation**

Proceed with the baseline plan, incorporating the following refinements:
1. Reduce hallucination target to <10% for pilot; keep <5% as Expand criterion.
2. Increase test set to 200 questions for statistical significance (95% CI ±4.2% at 10% error rate).
3. Pre-allocate $500 of contingency reserve as infrastructure backup if IT credits are not provided.
4. Faculty sponsor directly assists TA with privacy review during Weeks 6-8 to prevent TA overload.
5. Explicitly deprioritize documentation and non-critical features during Weeks 6-8.
6. Define "pass" for Week 7 UAT gate: correct response with source citation, no PII leakage, appropriate escalation.
7. Document backup plan if Dev A becomes unavailable: faculty takes over escalation design, TA handles basic bug fixes, timeline may slip 1 week.
8. Confirm student developer work hours (10-15/week) and faculty sponsor availability (4-6 hours/week during Weeks 6-8) in writing before Week 1.
9. Defer data collection prep to Week 9 to reduce TA overload during Weeks 6-8.
10. Require Dev A to document all critical code paths and architecture decisions by end of Week 5.

At Week 5, if Dev B becomes unavailable, execute the contingency overlay: reallocate $750 from infrastructure to privacy review and testing, shift ownership to Dev A and TA (with faculty assistance), and reduce infrastructure scope to free-tier tools. These adjustments preserve core functionality, strengthen safeguards, and stay within budget while addressing capacity constraints. The pilot remains viable for a 12-week evaluation, with clear decision criteria for expansion, revision, or termination.
